subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Right now, as you read this, invisible pushes and pulls are acting on you. Gravity is holding you down onto your chair. The chair is pushing back up so that you do not sink through it. If a cool breeze from the Indian Ocean blows through the window, you can feel the air pushing gently against your skin. Every one of these pushes and pulls is a force, and this unit is all about forces and the way they make things move, stop, and change shape.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 6 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what a force is and describe forces as pushes or pulls that have both size and direction.
Name the main types of force: gravity, friction, air resistance, water resistance, and pushes and pulls from contact.
Explain the difference between mass (measured in kilograms) and weight (measured in newtons).
Calculate the weight of an object using the rule weight = mass x gravity.
Describe how friction helps us and how it slows us down, and compare friction on sandy and paved roads.
Explain balanced and unbalanced forces and predict whether an object will speed up, slow down, or stay the same.
Measure forces in newtons using a forcemeter you build yourself.
Calculate speed using the rule speed = distance / time.
Give real examples of forces from farming, fishing, sport, and travel in East Africa.
Here are the most important words in this unit. Keep coming back to this table whenever a word confuses you.

concepts.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
concepts.explanation: Right now, as you read this, invisible pushes and pulls are acting on you. Gravity is holding you down onto your chair. The chair is pushing back up so that you do not sink through it. If a cool breeze from the Indian Ocean blows through the window, you can feel the air pushing gently against your skin. Every one of these pushes and pulls is a force, and this unit is all about forces and the way they make things move, stop, and change shape.
concepts.example: You already know a great deal about forces without ever using the word. When you help load a donkey cart in the market at Hargeisa, you feel how much harder you must pull when the cart is heavy. When you kick a football…

concepts.title: Forces and Motion — part 2
concepts.explanation: You already know a great deal about forces without ever using the word. When you help load a donkey cart in the market at Hargeisa, you feel how much harder you must pull when the cart is heavy. When you kick a football on a dusty pitch, you send it flying across the ground. When a ripe mango lets go of the branch and drops straight down to the earth, you are watching gravity at work. In this unit you will learn the scientific words and ideas that explain all of these everyday experiences. By the end you will be…
concepts.example: Forces matter far beyond the classroom. Engineers use forces to build bridges over the Jubba River that will not fall down. Sailors on the coast near Lamu and Mombasa use the force of the wind in a dhow sail to carry…

concepts.title: Forces and Motion — part 3
concepts.explanation: Forces matter far beyond the classroom. Engineers use forces to build bridges over the Jubba River that will not fall down. Sailors on the coast near Lamu and Mombasa use the force of the wind in a dhow sail to carry cargo across the sea. Doctors understand the forces on our bones and muscles. Even the pilots who fly aeroplanes into Mogadishu depend on forces of lift and thrust. Learning about forces is learning about how our whole world works. As you study, remember that observing creation carefully and asking…
concepts.example: Ask Your AI Tutor Tell your AI tutor one thing you moved today - a bucket of water, a school bag, a goat that would not walk. Ask: What forces was I using when I moved that object? Was I pushing or pulling? Your tutor…

concepts.title: Forces and Motion — part 4
concepts.explanation: Ask Your AI Tutor Tell your AI tutor one thing you moved today - a bucket of water, a school bag, a goat that would not walk. Ask: What forces was I using when I moved that object? Was I pushing or pulling? Your tutor can help you spot forces in the ordinary things you do every day.

explorations.title: Build Your Own Forcemeter
explorations.context: To build a simple forcemeter from a rubber band and use it to compare the weights of small objects.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down your prediction: which of your chosen objects do you think will stretch the rubber band the most, and why? A heavier object has a greater weight, so it should pull the band down further.
explorations.answer: Which object stretched the rubber band the most? What does that tell you about its weight? Put your objects in order from lightest to heaviest using only your stretch measurements. Explain in your own words why a greater weight makes the rubber band stretch further. Why is it important that the…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a simple forcemeter from a rubber band and use it to compare the weights of small objects.

explorations.title: The Friction Ramp
explorations.context: To find out how different surfaces change the amount of friction acting on a small object as it slides down a ramp.
explorations.prompt: Predict which surface will have the most friction and which will have the least. Which surface do you think will let the object slide down most easily? Write your reason before you begin.
explorations.answer: Which surface needed the ramp to be raised the most before the object slid? What does that tell you about its friction? Which surface had the least friction? How could you tell? This is like the difference between a sandy road and a paved road. Which surface in your test behaves like the sandy…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out how different surfaces change the amount of friction acting on a small object as it slides down a ramp.

explorations.title: Racing Falling Objects (Air Resistance)
explorations.context: To discover how air resistance changes the way objects fall, using two identical sheets of paper.
explorations.prompt: You will drop two sheets of paper that have exactly the same mass, but one is crushed into a ball and one is left flat. Predict which will land first, and write down why you think so.
explorations.answer: The two sheets of paper have the same mass. Why did the crushed ball fall faster than the flat sheet? Which object met more air resistance as it fell? How does the shape explain this? How is the flat sheet of paper like a parachute? What does the parachute do for a person jumping from a plane? If…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To discover how air resistance changes the way objects fall, using two identical sheets of paper.

explorations.title: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces Tug Test
explorations.context: To feel the difference between balanced and unbalanced forces using a simple pull test.
explorations.prompt: Predict what will happen to a small object when two equal pulls act on it in opposite directions, and what will happen when one pull is stronger than the other.
explorations.answer: When both pulls were equal and opposite, did the object move? Were the forces balanced or unbalanced? When one pull was stronger, which way did the object move - toward the stronger or the weaker pull? Why? For the book resting on the table, name the two balanced forces and the direction each one…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To feel the difference between balanced and unbalanced forces using a simple pull test.

methods.title: Build Your Own Forcemeter
methods.example: To build a simple forcemeter from a rubber band and use it to compare the weights of small objects.
steps: Tie or clip the cup to the bottom of the rubber band so it hangs down.
Hold the top of the rubber band still, or hook it over a nail or a door handle so it hangs freely.
Hold the ruler beside the rubber band. Note the starting position of the bottom of the cup when it is empty. This is your zero mark.
Place one object in the cup. Measure how far the bottom of the cup drops from the zero mark, in centimetres. Record it.
Remove the object and check the band returns to the zero mark. Then test the next object the same way.

methods.title: The Friction Ramp
methods.example: To find out how different surfaces change the amount of friction acting on a small object as it slides down a ramp.
steps: Prop up one end of the ramp on a book so it makes a gentle slope. Keep the height the same for every test - this is your fair test rule.
Lay the first surface (for example, smooth plastic) flat along the ramp.
Place your object at the top and let go without pushing. Watch whether it slides easily, slides slowly, or does not slide at all.
If it does not slide, add one more book to raise the ramp until it just begins to slide. Record how many books were needed.
Repeat with each different surface, always starting from a flat ramp and adding books one at a time.

methods.title: Racing Falling Objects (Air Resistance)
methods.example: To discover how air resistance changes the way objects fall, using two identical sheets of paper.
steps: Hold the two flat sheets of paper, one in each hand, at exactly the same height above the floor.
Drop them both at the same moment and watch how they fall. Record what you see.
Now crush one sheet tightly into a small ball. Leave the other sheet flat.
Hold both at the same height again and drop them together. Watch carefully and record which lands first.
Repeat two or three times to make sure your result is not just chance.

methods.title: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces Tug Test
methods.example: To feel the difference between balanced and unbalanced forces using a simple pull test.
steps: Place the small object on a smooth floor. Tie a string to each side, or hold the object gently between two fingers.
With a helper, both pull the string in opposite directions with equal, gentle force. Watch whether the object moves. Record it.
Now have one person pull a little harder than the other. Watch which way the object moves. Record it.
Try a solo version: rest a book on the table (gravity down, table up) and note that it stays still because forces are balanced.
Give the same book a gentle push so it slides and then stops. Note that an unbalanced push started it and friction stopped it.

workedExamples.title: Calculating Weight
workedExamples.prompt: Let us practise the weight rule together, one careful step at a time. Read each example slowly and make sure you understand every line before moving on. Keep the rule in front of you: Weight = mass x gravity, and on Earth gravity g = 10 N/kg.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: The bag of rice Amina buys a bag of rice with a mass of 5 kg in the market at Garissa. What is its weight on Earth? Write down what you know. Mass m = 5 kg. Gravity g = 10 N/kg. Write the rule. Weight = mass x gravity.

workedExamples.title: 1: The bag of rice
workedExamples.prompt: Amina buys a bag of rice with a mass of 5 kg in the market at Garissa. What is its weight on Earth?
workedExamples.solution: Write down what you know. Mass m = 5 kg. Gravity g = 10 N/kg. Write the rule. Weight = mass x gravity. Put the numbers in. Weight = 5 x 10. Do the multiplication. Weight = 50.

workedExamples.title: 2: The water container
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan fills a plastic jerrycan with water. The full jerrycan has a mass of 20 kg. What is its weight?
workedExamples.solution: What you know: mass m = 20 kg, gravity g = 10 N/kg. Rule: Weight = mass x gravity. Substitute: Weight = 20 x 10. Calculate: Weight = 200.

workedExamples.title: 3: Working backwards to find mass
workedExamples.prompt: Sometimes you know the weight and must find the mass. A goat weighs 300 N on Earth. What is its mass?
workedExamples.solution: What you know: weight W = 300 N, gravity g = 10 N/kg. Rearrange the rule. Since Weight = mass x gravity, we get mass = weight / gravity. Substitute: mass = 300 / 10. Calculate: mass = 30.

workedExamples.title: 4: Finding the net force
workedExamples.prompt: A football is being blown by the wind. The wind pushes it to the right with a force of 12 N. A child kicks it to the left with a force of 20 N. What is the net force, and which way does the ball go?
workedExamples.solution: List the forces. Wind: 12 N to the right. Kick: 20 N to the left. The forces are in opposite directions, so subtract. 20 - 12 = 8. Choose the direction of the larger force. The kick (20 N) is larger, so the net force points left. Answer: The net force is 8 N to the left, so the ball moves to the left.

workedExamples.title: 5: The speed of a goat
workedExamples.prompt: A goat trots 20 metres across a field in 10 seconds. What is its speed?
workedExamples.solution: Write what you know. Distance = 20 m. Time = 10 s. Write the rule. Speed = distance / time. Substitute the numbers. Speed = 20 / 10. Calculate. Speed = 2.

workedExamples.title: 6: A football on the pitch
workedExamples.prompt: Farah kicks a football and it rolls 30 metres in 6 seconds before stopping. What was its average speed?
workedExamples.solution: What you know: distance = 30 m, time = 6 s. Rule: Speed = distance / time. Substitute: Speed = 30 / 6. Calculate: Speed = 5.

workedExamples.title: 7: A journey by bus
workedExamples.prompt: A bus travels 120 kilometres from Mombasa toward Nairobi in 2 hours. What is its average speed?
workedExamples.solution: What you know: distance = 120 km, time = 2 h. Rule: Speed = distance / time. Substitute: Speed = 120 / 2. Calculate: Speed = 60.

practice.prompt: A force is best described as: (a) a colour (b) a push or a pull (c) a type of metal (d) a kind of light
practice.answer: 1 b A force is a push or a pull. It is neither a colour, a metal, nor a kind of light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The unit used to measure force is the: (a) kilogram (b) metre (c) newton (d) litre
practice.answer: 2 c Force is measured in newtons (N). Kilograms measure mass; metres measure length.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The force that pulls a mango down toward the ground is: (a) friction (b) gravity (c) air resistance (d) tension
practice.answer: 3 b Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward the centre of the Earth.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Mass is measured in: (a) newtons (b) kilograms (c) joules (d) seconds
practice.answer: 4 b Mass is the amount of matter and is measured in kilograms.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A 4 kg object on Earth (g = 10 N/kg) has a weight of: (a) 4 N (b) 14 N (c) 40 N (d) 400 N
practice.answer: 5 c Weight = mass x gravity = 4 x 10 = 40 N.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Friction always acts: (a) in the same direction as motion (b) against the direction of motion (c) upward only (d) only in water
practice.answer: 6 b Friction always acts against the direction of motion, slowing objects down.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which road gives a donkey cart the most friction? (a) a smooth paved road (b) a soft sandy track (c) a wet tiled floor (d) a sheet of ice
practice.answer: 7 b A soft sandy track grips the wheels most, giving the greatest friction.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A parachute works because it creates a large amount of: (a) gravity (b) mass (c) air resistance (d) friction with the ground
practice.answer: 8 c A parachute traps air and creates large air resistance to slow the fall.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Forces that are equal in size and opposite in direction are called: (a) unbalanced (b) balanced (c) magnetic (d) gravitational
practice.answer: 9 b Equal and opposite forces are balanced forces.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When forces on an object are unbalanced, the object will: (a) stay exactly the same (b) change its motion (c) lose its mass (d) become lighter
practice.answer: 10 b Unbalanced forces always change an object's motion.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The tool used to measure force in newtons is a: (a) thermometer (b) ruler (c) forcemeter (d) clock
practice.answer: 11 c A forcemeter (spring scale) measures force in newtons.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Speed is calculated using the rule: (a) mass x gravity (b) distance / time (c) force x distance (d) time x mass
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Build Your Own Forcemeter
activities.materials: One thin rubber band (an elastic band); A ruler or a strip of card marked in centimetres; A paper or plastic cup, or a small bag, to hold objects; String or a paper clip to hang the cup; Small objects to test: a spoon, a stone, a few coins, a small fruit such as a lime or a small mango; A pencil…
steps: Tie or clip the cup to the bottom of the rubber band so it hangs down.
Hold the top of the rubber band still, or hook it over a nail or a door handle so it hangs freely.
Hold the ruler beside the rubber band. Note the starting position of the bottom of the cup when it is empty. This is your zero mark.
Place one object in the cup. Measure how far the bottom of the cup drops from the zero mark, in centimetres. Record it.
Remove the object and check the band returns to the zero mark. Then test the next object the same way.
Safety: Pins and nails have sharp points — ask an adult to help, and store them safely afterwards.

activities.title: The Friction Ramp
activities.materials: A flat board, a stiff book cover, or a wooden tray to use as a ramp; Some books to prop up one end of the ramp; A small object that slides, such as a coin, an eraser, or a small block; Different surface materials to lay on the ramp: a smooth plastic sheet, a piece of cloth, a sheet of rough…
steps: Prop up one end of the ramp on a book so it makes a gentle slope. Keep the height the same for every test - this is your fair test rule.
Lay the first surface (for example, smooth plastic) flat along the ramp.
Place your object at the top and let go without pushing. Watch whether it slides easily, slides slowly, or does not slide at all.
If it does not slide, add one more book to raise the ramp until it just begins to slide. Record how many books were needed.
Repeat with each different surface, always starting from a flat ramp and adding books one at a time.

activities.title: Racing Falling Objects (Air Resistance)
activities.materials: Two identical sheets of paper of the same size; A small light object and a slightly heavier unbreakable object, such as a plastic bottle cap and a coin (optional second test); A clear space with no breakable items below; A notebook
steps: Hold the two flat sheets of paper, one in each hand, at exactly the same height above the floor.
Drop them both at the same moment and watch how they fall. Record what you see.
Now crush one sheet tightly into a small ball. Leave the other sheet flat.
Hold both at the same height again and drop them together. Watch carefully and record which lands first.
Repeat two or three times to make sure your result is not just chance.

activities.title: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces Tug Test
activities.materials: A length of string or a strong rubber band; A small light object such as a bottle cap or an eraser, or a toy on a smooth floor; A smooth floor or table top; A helper (a sibling, friend, or parent) for part of the test; A notebook
steps: Place the small object on a smooth floor. Tie a string to each side, or hold the object gently between two fingers.
With a helper, both pull the string in opposite directions with equal, gentle force. Watch whether the object moves. Record it.
Now have one person pull a little harder than the other. Watch which way the object moves. Record it.
Try a solo version: rest a book on the table (gravity down, table up) and note that it stays still because forces are balanced.
Give the same book a gentle push so it slides and then stops. Note that an unbalanced push started it and friction stopped it.

activities.title: Observe: Forces and Motion — part 5
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of forces and motion — part 5 around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Forces and Motion — part 6
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of forces and motion — part 6 around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: This Mass is the amount of matter in an object. It is measured in kilograms and never changes. Weight is the force of gravity on that mass. It is measured in newtons and changes with gravity. A kitchen scale measures mass in kilograms. A forcemeter (spring scale) measures weight in newtons.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: This Balanced forces mean NO change in motion. A still object stays still; a moving object keeps the same speed and direction. Unbalanced forces mean a CHANGE in motion. The object speeds up, slows down, or changes direction. It is the leftover, unbalanced part of the force - the net force - that changes how something moves.

terms: Weight = mass x gravity (W = m x g)
subtract the smaller from the larger
A force that opposes movement when two surfaces rub together.
A friction force from air pushing against a moving object; also called drag.
A force from water pushing against an object moving through it.
Equal and opposite forces that cancel out, so motion does not change.
Unequal forces that produce a net force and change an object's motion.
The single overall force left after all forces are added or subtracted.
How fast an object moves: distance divided by time.

vocabulary.meaning: Weight = mass x gravity (W = m x g)
vocabulary.example: By the end you will be able to measure forces, calculate weight and speed, and predict how objects will move.
Explain the difference between mass (measured in kilograms) and weight (measured in newtons).
By the end you will be able to measure forces, calculate weight and speed, and predict how objects will move.
When forces are unbalanced, there is an overall force - scientists call it a net force - pushing one way, and this net force changes the object's motion.

vocabulary.meaning: subtract the smaller from the larger
vocabulary.example: When forces are unbalanced, there is an overall force - scientists call it a net force - pushing one way, and this net force changes the object's motion.

vocabulary.meaning: A force that opposes movement when two surfaces rub together.
vocabulary.example: Describe how friction helps us and how it slows us down, and compare friction on sandy and paved roads.

vocabulary.meaning: A friction force from air pushing against a moving object; also called drag.
vocabulary.example: Big Idea 4: Air Resistance and Water Resistance

vocabulary.meaning: A force from water pushing against an object moving through it.
vocabulary.example: Big Idea 4: Air Resistance and Water Resistance

vocabulary.meaning: Equal and opposite forces that cancel out, so motion does not change.
vocabulary.example: Balanced forces are forces that are equal in size but pull or push in opposite directions, so they cancel each other out.

vocabulary.meaning: Unequal forces that produce a net force and change an object's motion.
vocabulary.example: Explain balanced and unbalanced forces and predict whether an object will speed up, slow down, or stay the same.

vocabulary.meaning: The single overall force left after all forces are added or subtracted.
vocabulary.example: When forces are unbalanced, there is an overall force - scientists call it a net force - pushing one way, and this net force changes the object's motion.

vocabulary.meaning: How fast an object moves: distance divided by time.
vocabulary.example: By the end you will be able to measure forces, calculate weight and speed, and predict how objects will move.

commonMistakes: Kilograms measure mass, not weight. Weight is a force.
It ignores air resistance. Shape, not just mass, decides how fast something falls in air.
It confuses starting motion with keeping motion.
It ignores how much we rely on grip.

connections.text: Next unit (Electricity): electric and magnetic forces are non-contact forces, just like gravity.
you used division for speed and multiplication for weight, and you added and subtracted forces.
friction on sandy versus paved roads affects travel and trade across East Africa.
forces explain how you kick, run, stop, and turn in football and other games.
streamlined shapes for dhows and boats reduce water resistance.
Watch how a dhow sail fills with wind at the coast and think about the pushing force it captures.

fluency.prompt: Speed is calculated using the rule: (a) mass x gravity (b) distance / time (c) force x distance (d) time x mass
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

realProblems.prompt: Use the rules and ideas from the Lesson book. Show every step in your calculations.
realProblems.answer: 1 Weight = mass x gravity = 30 x 10 = 300 N. Amina weighs 300 newtons on Earth.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1 Weight = mass x gravity = 30 x 10 = 300 N. Amina weighs 300 newtons on Earth.

realProblems.prompt: Amina has a mass of 30 kg. Calculate her weight on Earth (g = 10 N/kg). Show your working.
realProblems.answer: 2 mass = weight / gravity = 250 / 10 = 25 kg. The sack has a mass of 25 kilograms.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2 mass = weight / gravity = 250 / 10 = 25 kg. The sack has a mass of 25 kilograms.

realProblems.prompt: A sack of grain weighs 250 N on Earth. Calculate its mass. Show your working.
realProblems.answer: 3 Speed = distance / time = 90 / 1.5 = 60 km/h. The bus travels at 60 kilometres per hour.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3 Speed = distance / time = 90 / 1.5 = 60 km/h. The bus travels at 60 kilometres per hour.

realProblems.prompt: A bus travels 90 km from Berbera in 1.5 hours. Calculate its average speed in km/h.
realProblems.answer: 4 Idris and his friend push the same way: 90 + 70 = 160 N forward. Friction pushes back 120 N. Net force = 160 - 120 = 40 N forward. Because there is a leftover forward force, the cart starts moving forward.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4 Idris and his friend push the same way: 90 + 70 = 160 N forward. Friction pushes back 120 N. Net force = 160 - 120 = 40 N forward. Because there is a leftover forward force, the cart starts moving forward.

realProblems.prompt: Idris helps push a donkey cart. He pushes with 90 N and his friend pushes the same way with 70 N. Friction pushes back with 120 N. Calculate the net force and say whether the cart starts moving.
realProblems.answer: 5 Speed = distance / time = 60 / 40 = 1.5 m/s. The camel walks at 1.5 metres per second.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5 Speed = distance / time = 60 / 40 = 1.5 m/s. The camel walks at 1.5 metres per second.

realProblems.prompt: A camel walks 60 m across the savanna in 40 s. Calculate its speed in metres per second.
realProblems.answer: 6 Grease reduces the friction between the moving parts so they slide smoothly, wear out more slowly, and need less force to move.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 6 Grease reduces the friction between the moving parts so they slide smoothly, wear out more slowly, and need less force to move.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between mass and weight. Give the correct unit for each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1 Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms (kg), and it never changes. Weight is the force of gravity on that mass, measured in newtons (N), and it changes with gravity.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two forces that can slow a moving object down.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2 Friction and air resistance (or water resistance) both slow a moving object down. Any two of these are correct.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is friction greater on a sandy road than on a paved road?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3 Sand is loose and rough, so the wheels press into it and rub against many grains, creating more friction. A paved road is smooth and hard, so there is less friction.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one example where friction is helpful and one example where friction is a nuisance.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4 Helpful: friction between sandals and the ground lets you walk without slipping. Nuisance: friction between machine parts makes them wear out (other sensible examples are fine).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What does it mean when we say the forces on a resting book are balanced?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5 It means the downward pull of gravity and the upward push of the table are equal in size and opposite in direction, so there is no overall force and the book stays still.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe one way a force can change the shape of an object.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6 A force can squeeze (compress), stretch, bend, or twist an object. For example, squeezing dough or stretching a rubber band changes its shape.

questions.question: A force is best described as:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A force is a push or a pull. It is neither a colour, a metal, nor a kind of light.

questions.question: The unit used to measure force is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Force is measured in newtons (N). Kilograms measure mass; metres measure length.

questions.question: The force that pulls a mango down toward the ground is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the force that pulls objects toward the centre of the Earth.

questions.question: Mass is measured in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter and is measured in kilograms.

questions.question: A 4 kg object on Earth (g = 10 N/kg) has a weight of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Weight = mass x gravity = 4 x 10 = 40 N.

questions.question: Friction always acts:
options: in the same direction as motion
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Friction always acts against the direction of motion, slowing objects down.

questions.question: Which road gives a donkey cart the most friction?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A soft sandy track grips the wheels most, giving the greatest friction.

questions.question: A parachute works because it creates a large amount of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A parachute traps air and creates large air resistance to slow the fall.

questions.question: Forces that are equal in size and opposite in direction are called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Equal and opposite forces are balanced forces.

questions.question: When forces on an object are unbalanced, the object will:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Unbalanced forces always change an object's motion.

questions.question: The tool used to measure force in newtons is a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A forcemeter (spring scale) measures force in newtons.

questions.question: Speed is calculated using the rule:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.

selfAssessment: I can explain what a force is and describe forces as pushes or pulls that have both size and direction.
I can name the main types of force: gravity, friction, air resistance, water resistance, and pushes and pulls from contact.
I can explain the difference between mass (measured in kilograms) and weight (measured in newtons).
I can calculate the weight of an object using the rule weight = mass x gravity.
I can describe how friction helps us and how it slows us down, and compare friction on sandy and paved roads.
I can explain balanced and unbalanced forces and predict whether an object will speed up, slow down, or stay the same.
I can measure forces in newtons using a forcemeter you build yourself.
I can calculate speed using the rule speed = distance / time.
